nativeRange.habitat: Found on rock, coral, and rubble reef bottoms, generally from around 5 to 50 m depth, with records extending to approximately 138 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Hawaiian coral reef with abundant live rock for grazing and crevice cover

decorPreferences: open swimming space along the front

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on filamentous algae and detritus in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied diet of spirulina, nori and marine-algae preparations, high-quality angelfish formulas, mysis and enriched brine shrimp, fed multiple small meals per day. Newly imported specimens are often reluctant feeders; live or thawed meaty foods are commonly used to initiate feeding before weaning onto prepared foods.

goodWith: peaceful to semi-aggressive reef fish of differing body shape
avoidWith: other dwarf angelfish (Centropyge spp.) in typical home aquaria
conspecifics unless introduced as a known male-female pair
very docile slow feeders that may be out-competed

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by jeyre; 2048x1411. Tack-sharp full-body single specimen in situ, vivid orange body with blue-black vertical striations, electric-blue caudal fin and yellow pelvic fins clearly shown.
Photo by jeyre; 1690x1216. Whole fish in frame at a different angle, bright blue caudal fin and blue-black marbling on orange body; slight underwater blue cast.
Photo by Dr. Dwayne Meadows, NOAA/NMFS/OPR; 1472x1120. Sharp natural reef context; Potter's Angel at left shows diagnostic coloration well (shares frame with a goldring surgeonfish).

summary: Potter's Angelfish (Centropyge potteri) is a Hawaiian-endemic dwarf angelfish recognized by its vivid orange body marbled with blue-black markings. It is generally considered an advanced-level species due to its finicky feeding and tendency to nip at soft corals and clam mantles.
careNotes: Endemic to the Hawaiian Islands and Johnston Atoll, where it grazes algae and detritus over rocky reef bottoms. Generally advised to keep in a well-established system of at least around 280–300 L (75 US gal) with abundant live rock for grazing and multiple hiding places; sources vary from ~200 L on the low end to 280+ L on the cautious end, and a larger footprint is commonly recommended given the species' territorial behavior and need for swimming room. Water parameters are typical reef values (about 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12). Has been reported to be one of the more aggressive Centropyge species toward conspecifics and other dwarf angels, so it is generally kept singly or as an established pair. Reef compatibility is variable: many specimens have been documented nipping at large-polyp stony (LPS) corals, clam mantles, and zoanthids, so it is commonly considered 'reef safe with caution' rather than fully reef-safe. Newly imported individuals are often slow to accept prepared foods; live or thawed meaty items are commonly used to initiate feeding before transitioning to algae-based preparations. Like other Centropyge, it is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first) and forms monogamous home-ranging pairs in the wild, with no reliable external sexual dimorphism.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn pelagically at dusk in the week leading up to the full moon, with peak reproductive activity from mid-December through May in the wild. Considered very difficult to breed in home aquaria; rearing of pelagic larvae is generally not achieved outside specialist facilities. No reliable external sexual dimorphism, which complicates pair formation.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: tank size 70 US gal, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, care level difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes, max length 12.5 cm TL, Hawaiian/Johnston endemic, monogamous home-ranging pairs, oviparous pelagic dusk spawner.

additional.name: Reefapp encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (200 L low end), salinity SG 1.026, and reef-safety caveat ('reef safe with luck'). Noted disagreement with LiveAquaria on tank size; biased to larger value per playbook tank-size rule.

fish.breedingNotes: The Potter's Angelfish is a pelagic dusk spawner in the wild and has not been reliably bred in home aquaria. No reliable external sexual dimorphism has been documented to aid in distinguishing the sexes.
